explanation: First, identify the meaning in LTL: 'The system shall prompt the user before ventilation is started to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds for SAV.3- SAV.9.'   The atomic proposition corresponding to 'prompt the user ... to enter thresholds' is 'enterAlarmThresholds'. The proposition for 'ventilation is started' is 'ventilating'.   The requirement is: BEFORE ventilation starts, the system shall prompt the user to enter the thresholds. In LTL, 'before' can be modeled as: it should never happen that 'ventilating' becomes true before 'enterAlarmThresholds' becomes true. That is, at every point before the first 'ventilating', 'enterAlarmThresholds' must have already occurred. This can be captured by: 'It is always the case that if we have not started ventilation yet, then we have not yet ventilated, so eventually (before we ventilate) enterAlarmThresholds must happen.'   This is traditionally written as: '!ventilating U (enterAlarmThresholds & X F ventilating)' -- but more conventionally, it is captured as:  G((!ventilating) -> (!ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds)) -- this ensures that as long as ventilation has not started, eventually thresholds are entered before starting ventilation. Alternatively, a more succinct and common formalization is:  G(ventilating -> F_in_Past(enterAlarmThresholds)), but LTL is typically future-time only. So we settle with:  'It is always the case that if ventilation starts, then previously thresholds were entered.'  Or, equivalently, that 'ventilating' cannot occur until after 'enterAlarmThresholds' occurs:  (!ventilating) U (enterAlarmThresholds & X F ventilating)  However, for an always property, a standard safe LTL formalization is: 'It is always the case that if ventilation starts, then thresholds have already been entered.' Since LTL is strictly future time, we can express it as: G((!enterAlarmThresholds) -> !ventilating) -- but more generally: G(ventilating -> F_past(enterAlarmThresholds)), but we have no past in LTL-S.  So, the best future-time LTL is: 'The system shall not start ventilation until after thresholds are entered,' i.e.:  (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds  or, to ensure they are entered before the first ventilating:  G(ventilating -> F_past(enterAlarmThresholds)) but since we have no past in LTL, the safety property is: 'ventilating' cannot start until after 'enterAlarmThresholds'.  Final formula: (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds

explanation: The requirement states that the system must prompt the user before ventilation is started to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds. We can interpret this as: whenever ventilation is about to start (ventilating), it must be the case that before this happens, the user was prompted to enter alarm thresholds (represented by the atomic proposition enterAlarmThresholds). In LTL, 'before' can be expressed as: ventilation does not start unless prompts have occurred at some earlier point (i.e., it cannot be the case that ventilating occurs without a prior enterAlarmThresholds). A common LTL pattern for this is G(ventilating -> P enterAlarmThresholds), but since past operators like P are not allowed, we reformulate using standard LTL by requiring that always, if ventilating is true, then previously there was an enterAlarmThresholds. In standard LTL, this can be modeled as: it is not possible to reach ventilating before (without) enterAlarmThresholds, or G(!ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds), but this requires that enterAlarmThresholds must happen before ventilating becomes true at all. Alternatively, before each ventilating, an enterAlarmThresholds has to happen; this can be written as G((!ventilating) U (enterAlarmThresholds & X(ventilating))). This ensures that ventilating is always immediately preceded by a prompt to enter alarm thresholds. However, as LTL does not have direct past operators, the most faithful standard way is G(ventilating -> Once(enterAlarmThresholds)), which we approximate with G(ventilating -> F^-1 enterAlarmThresholds), but since F^-1 is not allowed, we ensure at every ventilating the previous step was enterAlarmThresholds: G(X(ventilating) -> enterAlarmThresholds). If we must ensure 'before *any* ventilating, prompt occurs', it can be written as: G((!ventilating) U (enterAlarmThresholds & X(ventilating))).

explanation: The requirement states that before ventilation (atomic proposition 'ventilating') is started (becomes true), the system shall prompt the user to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds (atomic proposition 'enterAlarmThresholds') for SAV.3-SAV.9. This is a typical 'prompt before action' pattern: whenever ventilation starts (i.e., 'ventilating' becomes true and was previously false), it must be preceded by the alarm threshold entry. In LTL, 'preceded by' is best represented as: Whenever 'ventilating' becomes true, 'enterAlarmThresholds' must have happened earlier (not strictly in the immediately previous step, but at least before). A simple and common way is to require that it is always the case that if ventilation is started for the first time, it must have been the case that 'enterAlarmThresholds' has occurred since the last time ventilation was not active. This can be translated as: G((!ventilating & X ventilating) -> F enterAlarmThresholds), but more generally and conservatively, to ensure 'enterAlarmThresholds' occurs before each segment of ventilation, we can say: 'It is always the case that entering ventilation implies that in the past, before this ventilation period, 'enterAlarmThresholds' occurred.' However, for simplicity, in finite LTL without past operators, a commonly accepted specification is: 'Whenever ventilation is started (i.e., transitions from false to true), at some earlier (or the same) point before that, enterAlarmThresholds has happened.' In standard LTL (without past), a widely readable pattern is: G((!ventilating & X ventilating) -> F (enterAlarmThresholds & !ventilating)). This means: globally, whenever the system is not ventilating now and ventilating in the next moment, at some point in the future (possibly next), there must be an 'enterAlarmThresholds' event while still not yet ventilating (i.e., before ventilation actually begins). This ensures the prompt occurs before ventilation.

But since afterwards ventilation may start, the safety template is: G(enterAlarmThresholds occurs before ventilating) = G(ventilating -> F_past enterAlarmThresholds), but as we lack past operators, we use: G(enterAlarmThresholds U ventilating) or more commonly, G(¬ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds) -> G(enterAlarmThresholds -> (¬ventilating U ventilating)). Ultimately, the most direct standard approach is: G(¬ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds), meaning it is always the case that ventilation cannot start before thresholds are entered. If this is not precise enough and we want to capture that every time before 'ventilating' becomes true, 'enterAlarmThresholds' must have occurred, the most common LTL is: G(ventilating -> F_past enterAlarmThresholds). Since no past operators (F_past) are allowed, we approximate with: '¬ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds'.

explanation:
To translate 'The system shall prompt the user before ventilation is started to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds for SAV.3- SAV.9' to an LTL formula, we need to identify atomic propositions:
- 'prompt the user ... to enter alarm thresholds' corresponds to 'enterAlarmThresholds'.
- 'ventilation is started' corresponds to 'ventilating'.
- 'before' here expresses a precedence; 'enterAlarmThresholds' must happen before 'ventilating'.
In LTL, a pattern for 'A occurs before B' is: 'It is always the case that B does not occur until after A has occurred.' That is, globally, if B occurs, then A must have happened in the past. Or, equivalently, before B, A occurs. In LTL (future-time only), we can say: '!ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds': it is not the case that ventilation (ventilating) happens until after the alarm thresholds are entered (enterAlarmThresholds). This ensures that ventilating is not true before enterAlarmThresholds becomes true. Alternatively, to be more precise and explicit: 'G(ventilating -> P enterAlarmThresholds)', but pure LTL does not enable past operators. So, the standard future-LTL encoding is:
'enterAlarmThresholds' must occur before 'ventilating' (i.e., 'ventilating' can't happen before 'enterAlarmThresholds'). This is expressed as: '!ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds'.
If 'ventilating' can happen multiple times, and each time we want 'enterAlarmThresholds' to happen before, then stricter would be: Each time 'ventilating' is started, 'enterAlarmThresholds' has already occurred. So:
G(ventilating -> F^(-1) enterAlarmThresholds)
But as we only have future-LTL, the typical choice is: '!ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds'.

explanation: First, 'before ventilation is started' means a condition that must be satisfied prior to when the system begins ventilating ('ventilating' becomes true). 'The system shall prompt the user ... to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds for SAV.3-SAV.9' can be abstractly formalized as 'enterAlarmThresholds' occurs before every start of ventilation. Thus, for every time ventilation begins (ventilating becomes true), it must have been preceded by the user entering alarm thresholds. Since LTL can't look backwards (past), we use an implication that says: it is always the case that if ventilation starts now (i.e., ventilating becomes true) then before that, enterAlarmThresholds already occurred. Typically, this is encoded as: globally, (if ventilating holds, then previously enterAlarmThresholds occurred), but standard LTL does not have the 'previous' operator. One common pattern is to require that 'enterAlarmThresholds' happens before the first occurrence of 'ventilating', and not after. Another encoding in standard LTL is: it is never the case that ventilating starts before enterAlarmThresholds has happened, which is expressed as 'not ventilating' until 'enterAlarmThresholds' holds: (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds. If there could be multiple ventilation episodes, then for every time ventilation is (re-)started, we would require 'enterAlarmThresholds' immediately before. If a one-time entry is sufficient, the formula '!ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds' is an adequate expression. For safety, we include a global: G(enterAlarmThresholds -> F ventilating), so that entering thresholds is eventually followed by ventilation. But the 'prompt before ventilation is started' most closely matches: 'it is always the case that ventilation cannot start until enterAlarmThresholds holds', i.e., (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds, and optionally, G(ventilating -> F past(enterAlarmThresholds)), or with only future operators, (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds.

explanation: The statement requires that before ventilation is started, the system must prompt the user to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds. Let's break this down: (1) 'ventilation is started' corresponds to the atomic proposition 'ventilating'. (2) 'system shall prompt the user … to enter alarm thresholds' maps to the atomic proposition 'enterAlarmThresholds'. (3) 'before ventilation is started' means every time ventilation starts, the alarm thresholds prompt must have already occurred. This is a classic 'pre-condition' property, and is commonly translated in LTL as: G(ventilating -> F(past enterAlarmThresholds)), but since LTL does not have past operators, we rephrase: whenever 'ventilating' becomes true, 'enterAlarmThresholds' must have occurred at some time earlier. In LTL over infinite traces, such precondition requirements are commonly weakened to 'ventilation does not start unless the prompt has occurred.' If we assume 'enterAlarmThresholds' occurs only at user prompt, then a suitable LTL formula is: G(ventilating -> F_past(enterAlarmThresholds)), but since we don't have past, we can express as: 'It is always the case that if ventilation starts, it must be preceded by the prompt at some point.' This is commonly approximated by: 'Until ventilation is started, enterAlarmThresholds must hold at least once.' If ventilation can start multiple times, then at every point where ventilation starts, the prompt must have occurred at some point before. The formula is thus best written as: G(ventilating -> P enterAlarmThresholds), but with no past, in standard LTL we ensure that at least before any ventilating, eventually the enterAlarmThresholds happens: !(ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds. But this only works for the first time ventilation starts. A robust way is: Globally, whenever 'ventilating' holds, then previously (or at least sometime before or at the same time), 'enterAlarmThresholds' held. A common workaround is to require that, after any state where enterAlarmThresholds is not set, ventilating cannot occur until enterAlarmThresholds. So, using LTL we can express: G((!enterAlarmThresholds) U enterAlarmThresholds -> !ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds). Alternatively, we can express: 'It is always the case that before ventilating becomes true, enterAlarmThresholds must have become true.' If we view enterAlarmThresholds as a required prerequisite, we use: G(ventilating -> F_past(enterAlarmThresholds)), which in standard LTL is: G((!enterAlarmThresholds) U enterAlarmThresholds -> (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds). To keep it executable and clear, a widely used pattern is: G(ventilating -> F (enterAlarmThresholds & X(ventilating))). But since our phrase is 'before ventilation is started', the best over-approximation is: 'At every time where ventilation starts to be true, previously alarm thresholds have been entered.' If the system only lets ventilation start after prompt, we can write: G((!ventilating & X(ventilating)) -> F_past(enterAlarmThresholds)), or without past, G((!ventilating & X(ventilating)) -> (enterAlarmThresholds | X(enterAlarmThresholds) | XX(enterAlarmThresholds)|...)). In pure LTL: we generally approximate by: 'It is always the case, it is not possible for ventilating to be true before enterAlarmThresholds.' This can be written: (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds. Once enterAlarmThresholds holds, ventilating can happen. So the formula is: (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds.

explanation: The key requirement is that before ventilation is started (ventilating), the system must prompt the user to enter alarm thresholds (enterAlarmThresholds) for SAV.3-SAV.9. Since SAV.3-SAV.9 is a group of alarm thresholds, we will use a single proposition 'enterAlarmThresholds' to indicate the act of prompting for all these thresholds. This temporal relationship can be captured as: whenever ventilation starts (in any state where ventilating becomes true), there must have previously been a state where the prompt (enterAlarmThresholds) occurred, and not vice versa (the prompt must not be after ventilation starts). In LTL, a common idiom for 'A must happen before B' is to require that B can only become true if A has happened at some previous point along the trace (with no B before the first A). We can express this as: 'It is always the case that if ventilation starts, then in the past (before now) the thresholds were entered.' But standard LTL does not have a past operator, so instead, we can require: 'It is always the case that if ventilating becomes true, then previously enterAlarmThresholds was true and there was no ventilating before that.' Since we are limited to standard (future-only) LTL, the safest and most accepted idiom is: 'It is always the case that ventilating is not true until after enterAlarmThresholds has occurred' (i.e., ¬ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds), and after the threshold entry, ventilation may happen at any point. To ensure the user is always prompted before each instance of ventilation, if ventilation can ever be off and then on again, it is safest to use: 'Whenever ventilating holds, it must have been preceded by enterAlarmThresholds.' That is, 'G(ventilating -> (F past enterAlarmThresholds))', but since we have no past operator, we must use 'enterAlarmThresholds' as a necessary precondition for 'ventilating'. So, 'G(ventilating -> P)', but with only future-based operators, we do: '¬ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds' ensures that ventilation cannot occur before thresholds are entered. If ventilating is never interrupted, this suffices. Otherwise, for recurring ventilation phases, we could require that each time ventilation is restarted, thresholds are reentered, but with the given atomic propositions, the most precise answer with standard LTL is: '¬ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds', or equivalently: 'G(ventilating -> (F enterAlarmThresholds))', but the first is stricter and better suited.

explanation: The requirement states: The system shall prompt the user before ventilation is started to enter user-controlled alarm thresholds for SAV.3-SAV.9. Let's identify the key events: 'prompt the user to enter alarm thresholds' can be mapped to the atomic proposition 'enterAlarmThresholds'. 'Ventilation is started' can be mapped to 'ventilating'. The phrase 'before ventilation is started' means that, whenever ventilation begins, the thresholds must have already been entered. In LTL, to express that 'enterAlarmThresholds' must happen before 'ventilating', we can use the 'until' (U) operator. More precisely, it should never be the case that 'ventilating' happens before 'enterAlarmThresholds' has occurred. Alternatively, we require that 'if ventilating happens, then previously enterAlarmThresholds was true.' In LTL, this is usually expressed as: G(!ventilating -> !ventilating U enterAlarmThresholds), meaning: as long as ventilation has not started, we must stay in "not ventilating" until 'enterAlarmThresholds' happens. Strictly, the customary way is to express that 'ventilation' cannot happen before 'enterAlarmThresholds' has occurred globally. Another equivalent standard formulation is: G(ventilating -> P enterAlarmThresholds), but as LTL does not have 'previous' (P), we commonly say that 'enterAlarmThresholds' must hold at or before the first 'ventilating'. Therefore, a frequently used idiom is: '!ventilating U (enterAlarmThresholds & X F ventilating)' meaning: we remain in "not ventilating" until we've entered thresholds and (in the next state) ventilation will eventually happen. However, the most accepted pattern is: G(ventilating -> F[<in past>] enterAlarmThresholds), and in regular LTL, to ensure 'enterAlarmThresholds' always occurs before 'ventilating', we use: !(ventilating & !F enterAlarmThresholds) or G(ventilating -> F(enterAlarmThresholds & !ventilating)). But, given the intent is to capture "prompt before ventilation is started", the standard (linear) way is: G(ventilating -> F past enterAlarmThresholds), in LTL: "It is never the case that ventilation is started before the thresholds have been entered", which is G((!enterAlarmThresholds) -> !ventilating), or more generally, 'enterAlarmThresholds' must happen before 'ventilating': (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds. To be explicit, G(ventilating -> F_past enterAlarmThresholds) can't be written in plain LTL, but the best we can do is G((!enterAlarmThresholds) -> !ventilating), or (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds. Therefore, the closest is: (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds. But to ensure that 'ventilating' can only happen after 'enterAlarmThresholds', we use: G(ventilating -> F_past enterAlarmThresholds), so in LTL, G(ventilating -> F(enterAlarmThresholds & !ventilating)). If we want to ensure that 'ventilating' does not happen before 'enterAlarmThresholds', a standard way is: G(ventilating -> F(enterAlarmThresholds)). But to demand that 'ventilating' only happens after 'enterAlarmThresholds', we use: (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds. To be crystal-clear, the standard pattern is: It is always the case that, before 'ventilating', we will observe 'enterAlarmThresholds', so (!ventilating) U enterAlarmThresholds. But, if ventilation may never start, we need to ensure that, whenever ventilation does start, the precondition is satisfied. Thus, the correct translation is: G(ventilating -> F_past enterAlarmThresholds). But since LTL without past operators cannot directly express 'in the past', the canonical safety property is: 'It is always the case that if not 'enterAlarmThresholds', then not 'ventilating'', i.e., G((!enterAlarmThresholds) -> !ventilating).
